figures.caption: Table 5: All 12 style groups and 41 style constraints in the coding subset. Constraints within the same group may conflict (e.g., indent_2 vs. indent_4); constraints across different groups are always compatible.

Figure 1: Overview of Many-Tier Instruction Hierarchy compared with existing IH. ManyIH enables arbitrary levels of privilege to be defined at inference based on instruction sources through a dedicated Privilege Prompt Interface (§3), tackling the fixed-tier bottleneck (§2) of existing instruction hierarchy.
Figure 2: Left: overall accuracy on MANYIH-BENCH. Right: accuracy by subset. Both frontier and open-source models struggle with ManyIH. Error bars show bootstrap 95% CIs.
Figure 3: Accuracy across IH tiers on the coding subset. Model performance consistently degrades as the number of IH tiers increases.

Figure 4: Analysis of reasoning behavior on the coding subset. (a) Claude and GPT produce the most concise reasoning (∼1K tokens), while Qwen 3.5-397B generates the longest (∼7K tokens median). (b) GPT-5.4 improves monotonically with reasoning effort, while Claude models exhibit a dip at low effort due to “thinking-out-loud” effect at None reasoning effort.
Table 1: Performance breakdown on the coding subset. (a) Overall accuracy, test correctness, and style compliance. (b) Change when switching from ordinal to scalar PPI.

Table 4: Statistics of the three coding subset configurations used in the IH tier scaling experiment (§6.2). Style groups and winning styles are held constant across variants; only the number of instructions, privilege levels, and conflicts increase. Input length is measured in whitespace-delimited words.

Table 3: Key statistics of the MANYIH-BENCH benchmark. The coding subset has fixed difficulty parameters by design, while the instruction-following subset has naturally varying complexity drawn from real agentic scenarios. Input length is measured in whitespacedelimited words across all messages in the model input.
